article.title: CERN Discovers a Heavy Cousin of the Proton, Only the Second Baryon With Two Charm Quarks Ever Observed
article.summary: The LHCb experiment identifies the Xicc+ baryon at 7-sigma significance, bringing the total hadrons found at the LHC to 80.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

The LHCb Collaboration at CERN's Large Hadron Collider has discovered a new subatomic particle composed of two charm quarks and one down quark, making it only the second baryon with two heavy quarks ever observed. The particle, designated Xicc+ (pronounced "ksai-cc-plus"), was presented on March 17, 2026, at the Moriond conference in La Thuile, Italy, and represents the first new particle identified since the LHCb detector completed its major upgrade in 2023, according to [CERN](https://home.cern/news/news/physics/lhcb-collaboration-discovers-new-proton-particle).

The discovery brings the total number of hadrons found by LHC experiments to 80.

## What We Know

The Xicc+ is structured similarly to a proton -- both are baryons made of three quarks -- but with two of the proton's light up quarks replaced by heavy charm quarks. This substitution makes the new particle approximately four times heavier than a proton, with a measured mass of 3,619.97 MeV/c-squared, as reported by [ScienceDaily](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/03/260319005106.htm).

Researchers identified the particle by reconstructing its decay products from proton-proton collision data collected during the LHC's third operational run in 2024. The Xicc+ decays into three lighter particles -- a lambda-c-plus, a kaon, and a pion -- and by measuring their combined invariant mass, the team observed a clear peak of approximately 915 events around 3,620 MeV/c-squared. The signal exceeded 7 sigma, well above the 5-sigma threshold required to claim a discovery in particle physics, according to [CERN](https://home.cern/news/news/physics/lhcb-collaboration-discovers-new-proton-particle).

The finding resolves a 20-year mystery. Earlier experimental searches had produced unconfirmed claims of the particle's existence, but none achieved the statistical significance needed for a definitive observation. The discovery was made possible by upgrades to the LHCb detector completed in 2023, which enhanced its data-collection capabilities for the LHC's third run, according to [ScienceDaily](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/03/260319005106.htm).

The Xicc+ is a close relative of the Xicc++ baryon, which was discovered by LHCb in 2017 and contains two charm quarks plus one up quark. The newly found particle, with a down quark instead, has a predicted lifetime up to six times shorter than its predecessor, according to [CERN](https://home.cern/news/news/physics/lhcb-collaboration-discovers-new-proton-particle).

## What We Don't Know

While the particle's mass has been precisely measured, its lifetime and other properties remain to be determined through further analysis. Additional LHC data will be needed to study the particle's behavior in detail and compare it with theoretical predictions from lattice quantum chromodynamics calculations.

It is also unclear whether the upgraded LHCb detector will reveal additional doubly heavy baryons -- particles containing two bottom quarks, or one charm and one bottom quark -- in the coming years. Such discoveries would provide further tests of the Standard Model's predictions for how the strong nuclear force operates in systems with multiple heavy quarks.

## Analysis

The discovery carries significance beyond cataloging another entry in the subatomic zoo. Baryons with two heavy quarks provide a testing ground for quantum chromodynamics (QCD), the theory governing the strong nuclear force that binds quarks into protons, neutrons, and other composite particles. LHCb Spokesperson Vincenzo Vagnoni noted that the result "will help theorists test models of quantum chromodynamics," according to [CERN](https://home.cern/news/news/physics/lhcb-collaboration-discovers-new-proton-particle).

In a system with two heavy quarks, the dynamics simplify enough for theorists to make more precise predictions, turning the particle into a benchmark against which competing models of quark interactions can be measured. Professor Chris Parkes drew a connection to the origins of nuclear physics, stating that the discovery "builds on that legacy" of probing the structure of matter, as reported by [ScienceDaily](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/03/260319005106.htm).

CERN Director-General Mark Thomson framed the result as validation of the laboratory's investment in detector technology, stating that "experimental upgrades at CERN directly lead to new discoveries," according to [Scientific American](https://www.scientificamerican.com/article/physicists-discover-a-charmed-new-particle/). With the LHC's third run ongoing and further upgrades planned, the experiment's hadron tally is likely to continue growing.
